questions.text: In the reaction, R-C≡C-R → the reagent used to convert alkyne into cis alkene is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hydrogen in the presence of catalysts such as Pd, Pt, and Ni causes full reduction. Alkynes are fully hydrogenated into alkanes and the alkene intermediate formed during the process (by the addition of hydrogen to alkyne) can’t be isolated.

To convert an alkyne into a cis alkene, the reagent commonly used is Lindlar's catalyst. Lindlar's catalyst consists of palladium (Pd) deposited on calcium carbonate (CaCO3) or barium sulfate (BaSO4) support, with quinoline or lead acetate as a poison to prevent further reduction to an alkane. Lindlar's catalyst selectively reduces the triple bond of an alkyne to a cis-alkene or trans-alkene, depending on the reaction conditions. By using Lindlar's catalyst under appropriate conditions (such as low temperature and low hydrogen pressure), cis-alkenes can be obtained. The reaction mechanism involves the partial reduction of the alkyne, stopping at the alkene stage. The presence of the poison helps to hinder the complete reduction to an alkane and directs the selectivity towards the cis-alkene product. Therefore, Lindlar's catalyst is the reagent commonly employed to convert an alkyne into a cis alkene.
When alkyne is reacted with borane followed by hot acetic acid cis-alkene is formed.

Alkynes can be reduced to trans alkenes by birch reduction using Li or Na dissolved in ammonia. This is also called anti-addition of hydrogen or dissolving metal reduction and involves radicals in its mechanism.
Alkynes don’t react with benzene.

questions.explanation: To convert an alkyne into a cis alkene, the reagent commonly used is Lindlar's catalyst. Lindlar's catalyst consists of palladium (Pd) deposited on calcium carbonate (CaCO3) or barium sulfate (BaSO4) support, with quinoline or lead acetate as a poison to prevent further reduction to an alkane. Lindlar's catalyst selectively reduces the triple bond of an alkyne to a cis-alkene or trans-alkene, depending on the reaction conditions. By using Lindlar's catalyst under appropriate conditions (such as low temperature and low hydrogen pressure), cis-alkenes can be obtained. The reaction mechanism involves the partial reduction of the alkyne, stopping at the alkene stage. The presence of the poison helps to hinder the complete reduction to an alkane and directs the selectivity towards the cis-alkene product. Therefore, Lindlar's catalyst is the reagent commonly employed to convert an alkyne into a cis alkene.

questions.text: Chlorine in Vinyl chloride is not very reactive because
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: C−Cl bond develops partial double bonds
options.explanation: In vinyl chloride, the C−Cl bond exhibits some degree of double bond character due to resonance. This is because the carbon atom involved in the C−Cl bond is sp2 hybridized and forms a pi (π) bond with the chlorine atom. The pi bond contributes to the stability of the molecule and reduces the reactivity of the C−Cl bond compared to a typical single bond.

Resonance refers to the delocalization of electrons within a molecule. While resonance does occur in vinyl chloride, it is more directly related to the partial double bond character of the C−Cl bond, as explained in option (a).

options.text: Sp2 hybridized carbon has more acidic character than sp3 hybridized carbon
options.explanation: This statement is generally not true. The acidity of a carbon atom is primarily determined by the stability of the conjugate base that forms after losing a proton. The sp2 hybridized carbon in vinyl chloride does not significantly affect its acidity in the context of the given question.

This option is not correct because the main reason for the low reactivity of chlorine in vinyl chloride is the development of partial double bond character (option a), rather than resonance (option b) or the acidic character of the carbon atom (option c).

questions.explanation: The chlorine in vinyl chloride is not very reactive because the C−Cl bond develops partial double bonds due to the pi bond formed between the sp2 hybridized carbon and the chlorine atom. This partial double bond character reduces the reactivity of the C−Cl bond.

questions.text: Which of the following is not acidic in nature ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethyne is a terminal alkyne and terminal alkynes are acidic. Hence, this is not the correct option.
Propyne is a terminal alkyne and terminal alkynes are acidic. Hence, this is not the correct option.
1-butyne is a terminal alkyne and terminal alkynes are acidic. Hence, this is not the correct option.

2-Butyne is an internal alkyne, meaning it has a triple bond within the carbon chain rather than at the end. Only terminal alkynes are acidic and since ,it is not a terminal alkyne ,it is not acidic. Hence, it is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: Terminal alkynes (ethyne, propyne, 1-butyne) have an acidic hydrogen on the sp-hybridized carbon. 2-Butyne is an internal alkyne with no hydrogen on the triple-bonded carbon. Therefore, 2-butyne is not acidic.

questions.text: Which of the following compound reacts with sodium to yield hydrogen gas ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is ethane, which is an alkane. Alkanes do not react with sodium to produce hydrogen gas, as they lack the necessary triple bond for such a reaction. Therefore, this is not the correct option.
This is propene, which is an alkene. Alkenes do not react with sodium to produce hydrogen gas because they do not have a triple bond. Therefore, this is not the correct option.

This is ethyne (acetylene), which is an alkyne. Alkynes react with sodium to produce sodium acetylide and hydrogen gas, as shown in the reaction: HC≡CH + 2Na ----> Na+C-≡C-Na+ + H2. Hence, this is the correct option.

This compound is butyne, which is also an alkyne. While it can react with sodium to produce hydrogen gas, the question asks for a specific compound. Since ethyne is the simplest and most commonly referenced example, this is not the best choice for the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The hydrogen atoms in terminal alkynes, like ethyne (acetylene), are sufficiently acidic to react with strong bases or active metals like sodium. This reaction displaces the acidic hydrogen as hydrogen gas.

questions.explanation: In the structure of an ethyne molecule, there is one sigma and two pi bonds. The two pi bonds are formed by the parallel overlapping of electrons above and below the C atoms. questions.text: How many unhybridized p orbitals are there in ethyne molecule?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Since 4 is the correct answer hence this option is incorrect.
Since 4 is the correct answer hence this option is incorrect.
Since 4 is the correct answer hence this option is incorrect.

In the ethyne molecule (C2H2), each carbon atom is sp hybridized to form a triple bond between them. The triple bond consists of one sigma (σ) bond and two pi (π) bonds.The sp hybridization involves one s orbital and one p orbital of each carbon atom combining to form two sp hybrid orbitals. These two sp hybrid orbitals overlap with each other to form a sigma (σ) bond, while the remaining two p orbitals on each carbon atom remain unhybridized and form two pi (π) bonds.So, in the ethyne molecule (C2H2), there are four unhybridized p orbitals - two on each carbon atom. These p orbitals participate in the formation of the two pi (π) bonds in the triple bond between the carbon atoms.

questions.explanation: In the ethyne molecule (C2H2), each carbon atom is sp hybridized to form a triple bond between them. The triple bond consists of one sigma (σ) bond and two pi (π) bonds. The sp hybridization involves one s orbital and one p orbital of each carbon atom combining to form two sp hybrid orbitals. These two sp hybrid orbitals overlap with each other to form a sigma (σ) bond, while the remaining two p orbitals on each carbon atom remain unhybridized and form two pi (π) bonds. So, in the ethyne molecule (C2H2), there are four unhybridized p orbitals - two on each carbon atom. These p orbitals participate in the formation of the two pi (π) bonds in the triple bond between the carbon atoms.

questions.text: Which method is generally used to prepare alkynes?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Dehydrohalogenation is a reaction where a vicinal dihalide (a compound with two halogen atoms on adjacent carbon atoms) is converted into an alkyne. This is typically done using a strong base, such as potassium hydroxide (KOH), to remove two molecules of hydrogen halide (HX) and form a triple bond between the carbon atoms.The general reaction is:RCHX-CHXR' → R-C≡C-R' + 2HXThis method is commonly used to prepare alkynes because it allows for the formation of the triple bond with good yields and selectivity.
Hydrogenation of alkene would add hydrogen to the double bond, forming an alkane, not an alkyne.
Hydrolysis would add water to the molecule, not form a triple bond.
Hydration of alkene would add water to the double bond, forming an alcohol, not an alkyne.

questions.explanation: Dehydrohalogenation is a reaction where a vicinal dihalide (a compound with two halogen atoms on adjacent carbon atoms) is converted into an alkyne. This is typically done using a strong base, such as potassium hydroxide (KOH), to remove two molecules of hydrogen halide (HX) and form a triple bond between the carbon atoms.The general reaction is:RCHX-CHXR' → R-C≡C-R' + 2HXThis method is commonly used to prepare alkynes because it allows for the formation of the triple bond with good yields and selectivity.

questions.text: Which compound is the most reactive one?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethyne is less reactive than ethene because it is an alkyne. Alkynes are more reactive than alkanes but less reactive than alkenes due to the presence of a triple bond, which is less reactive in addition reactions than the double bond in alkenes.
Ethane is the least reactive among the given options because it is an alkane. Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons and are very stable, making them less reactive in comparison to alkenes and alkynes.
Benzene, although aromatic and stable, is less reactive in addition reactions compared to alkenes. Its stability is due to resonance, which makes it require more severe conditions to react.

Ethene is the most reactive compound among the options because it is an alkene. Alkenes have a double bond that is highly reactive in addition reactions, making them more reactive than alkynes and alkanes.

questions.explanation: Explanation:Correct answer is Ethene because the general order of reactivity is as follows: Alkene > Alkyne > AlkaneWhile Benzene is unique in its behavior though resembles alkenes in addition reactions but to high stability of the molecule it requires dramatic conditions for substitution reactions in order to generate electrophile.

questions.text: In the CH3CH2C = CH + H2O ->?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option suggests the formation of two aldehyde molecules, which is not a typical result of hydration of an alkyne.

This option suggests the formation of a carboxylic acid, which is not typical for alkyne hydration reactions without further oxidation.
This option is correct because the hydration of the alkyne leads to the formation of a ketone, which is consistent with the addition of water across the triple bond.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D:CH3CH2COCH3. When water is added to an alkyne, it typically undergoes a hydration reaction resulting in the formation of aldehydes or ketones. In this case, the terminal alkyne undergoes hydration to form a ketone. Options A, B, and C suggest products that are not typically formed under these reaction conditions. Option A suggests aldehyde formation from a non-terminal alkyne, which is incorrect. Option B suggests alcohol formation, and option C suggests carboxylic acid formation, both of which are incorrect for this reaction.

questions.text: Kolbe's electrolytic method is used to prepare ethyne. Which of the following salts of carboxylic acid is used for this purpose?

Potassium maleate is the correct salt of carboxylic acid used in Kolbe's electrolytic method to prepare ethyne. The electrolysis of potassium maleate produces ethyne.

The electrolysis of potassium maleate results in the production of ethyne. The other options, sodium acetate, sodium succinate, and sodium oxalate, are salts of different carboxylic acids and are not the salts used in the Kolbe's electrolytic method.

questions.text: Alkynes show _ reactions.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Alkynes are capable of undergoing both electrophilic addition and nucleophilic addition reactions.

This option is incorrect. Alkynes are capable of undergoing both electrophilic addition and nucleophilic addition reactions, depending on the reaction conditions and the nature of the reagent involved.
This option is incorrect. Alkynes can participate in both electrophilic and nucleophilic addition reactions.

Correct! Alkynes are capable of undergoing both electrophilic addition and nucleophilic addition reactions, depending on the reaction conditions and the nature of the reagent involved. The triple bond in alkynes makes them reactive towards both electrophiles and nucleophiles, leading to the formation of various products.

questions.explanation: Alkynes are capable of undergoing both electrophilic addition and nucleophilic addition reactions. The presence of a triple bond in alkynes allows them to react with both electrophiles and nucleophiles. In electrophilic addition, the π electrons of the triple bond act as nucleophiles attacking electrophilic reagents, while in nucleophilic addition, a nucleophile attacks the positively polarized carbon atom in the alkyne. The correct answer option, 'Both electrophilic and nucleophilic addition,' is supported by the fact that alkynes can participate in both types of addition reactions, leading to the formation of various products. The other options are incorrect because alkynes can undergo both types of addition reactions, not just one exclusively.

questions.text: The boiling and melting points of alkynes -------- as their molecular structure becomes more complex.

options.explanation: As the molecular structure of the alkyne becomes more complex, the molecular weight increases, leading to stronger London dispersion forces. This results in higher energy required to overcome these forces, causing an increase in the melting and boiling point of alkynes.

Incorrect. As the molecular structure of the alkyne becomes more complex, the molecular weight increases, resulting in stronger London dispersion forces. This leads to higher energy required to overcome these forces, causing an increase in the melting and boiling point of alkynes, not a decrease.

This results in higher energy required to overcome these forces, causing an increase in the melting and boiling point of alkynes, not remaining constant.

Incorrect. As the molecular structure of the alkyne becomes more complex, the molecular weight increases, resulting in stronger London dispersion forces. This leads to higher energy required to overcome these forces, causing an increase in the melting and boiling point of alkynes, not fluctuation.

questions.explanation: When the molecular structure of alkynes becomes more complex, the molecular weight increases, leading to stronger London dispersion forces. This requires more energy to overcome these forces, resulting in an increase in the melting and boiling points of alkynes. Options B, C, and D are incorrect as they do not consider the relationship between molecular complexity and boiling/melting points correctly.

questions.text: Alkynes have a _ bonding strength
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alkynes do not have polar bonding strength, as they do not dissolve in polar reagents and only contain carbon and hydrogen, making them non-polar.
The hybridisation of Alkynes contributes to their non-polar nature, making them highly soluble in non-polar reagents and organic compounds, and only slightly soluble in polar compounds.
Alkynes have non-polar bonding strength, so the correct option is 'Non polar'. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
Alkynes have non-polar bonding strength, making one of the options correct. This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The hybridisation of Alkynes contributes to their non-polar nature, as they are highly soluble in non-polar reagents and organic compounds, and only slightly soluble in polar compounds. questions.text: Butyne and 2-Butyne can be identified by using which of the following reagents?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Oxygen will carry out addition reaction with them, but it is not specific enough to differentiate between butyne and 2-butyne. This option does not aid in the specific identification process of the two compounds.

Br2 will carry out addition reaction with both compounds, but it does not aid in the specific identification of butyne and 2-butyne. This option does not provide a method for distinguishing between the two compounds.

1-Butyne and 2-butyne are distinguished by NaNH2 because 1-butyne reacts with NaNH2 due to the presence of terminal hydrogen. This reagent specifically helps in identifying the differences between butyne and 2-butyne.

HCl is not directly used for the identification of specific compounds like butyne and 2-butyne. Specialized analytical techniques are necessary for accurate identification. This option is not relevant for differentiating between the two compounds.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: NaNH2. 1-Butyne and 2-butyne can be differentiated using NaNH2 due to the reactivity of terminal hydrogen. Option A and Option B do not provide specific methods for distinguishing between the two compounds, while Option D is not relevant for this identification process.

questions.text: Which has shortest C-C bond length:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In Ethyne (C2H2), the C-C bond is the shortest because both C atoms form a triple bond with each other, increasing bond energy and decreasing bond length. The bond length in alkynes is 1.20 Angstroms.
Option B contains an alkene with a bond length of 1.33 Angstroms.
Option C contains carbon tetrachloride with a bond length of 1.77 Angstroms.
Option D contains an alcohol (ethanol) with a bond length of 1.51 Angstroms.

questions.explanation: In Ethyne (C2H2), the C-C bond is the shortest because both C atoms form a triple bond with each other, increasing bond energy and decreasing bond length. The bond length in alkynes is 1.20 Angstroms. This makes option A the correct answer. Options B, C, and D have longer bond lengths compared to the triple bond in ethyne, making them incorrect choices.

questions.text: Compounds having benzene ring are called as _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alicyclic compounds are organic compounds that are both aliphatic and cyclic. These are the saturated or unsaturated hydrocarbons containing non-aromatic rings of carbon atoms. Alicyclic compounds may have one or more aliphatic side chains attached.

Aliphatic compounds can be saturated like hexane, or unsaturated, like hexene and hexyne. Open-chain compounds, whether straight or branched, and which contain no rings of any type, are always aliphatic.

Aromatic compounds, also known as "mono- and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons", are organic compounds containing one or more aromatic rings or benzene ring. The word "aromatic" originates from the past grouping of molecules based on smell, before their general chemical properties were understood.
An acyclic compound is a compound which do not have cyclic structure.

questions.explanation: Correct option is C. Aromatic compounds, also known as "mono- and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons", are organic compounds containing one or more aromatic rings. The word "aromatic" originates from the past grouping of molecules based on smell, before their general chemical properties were understood.

questions.text: Benzene molecule contains:

Three double bonds present between carbons ( alternatively in hexagonal ring). So a total of nine single bonds, three double bonds are present in the benzene ring.

questions.text: What is the hybridization state of carbon in benzene?

options.explanation: In benzene, the carbon atom is joined to two other similar carbon atoms instead of just one. Each carbon atom uses the sp2 hybrids to form sigma bonds with two other carbons and one hydrogen atom.

questions.text: Which of the following elements is needed to add in the given diagram to make it aromatic benzene?

options.explanation: The addition of a single hydrogen atom (-H) is necessary to satisfy the valency of a carbon atom, completing the structure of an aromatic benzene ring.

The addition of H2 is unnecessary and would result in an excess hydrogen, disrupting the aromatic stability of benzene.

questions.text: The resonance energy of benzene is :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Difference between amount of heat actually released and that calculated on the basis of the Kekule’s structure is called the ‘Resonance energy’ of the compound.The resonance energy of benzene is 150.5 kJ/mol as: Calculated ΔH = (3 x -119.5) =-358.5 kJ/moleObserved ΔH = -208 kJ/moleDifference = 150.5 kJ/mole Hence, this is not the correct option.

questions.text: Benzene-1,2-dicarboxylic acid is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Phenol is a different compound, consisting of a hydroxyl group (-OH) bonded directly to a benzene ring.
Benzene-1,2-dicarboxylic acid is commonly known as phthalic acid. It has two carboxylic acid groups (-COOH) attached to adjacent carbon atoms on a benzene ring.
Toluene is a different compound, an aromatic hydrocarbon with a methyl group (-CH3) attached to a benzene ring.
Aniline is an organic compound consisting of a benzene ring attached to an amino group (-NH2).

questions.explanation: The structure of benzene-1,2-dicarboxylic acid or phthalic acid, a benzene derivative, is as follows: Aniline, toluene, and phenol have molecular formulae C₆H₅NH₂, C₆H₅OH, and C6H5CH3 respectively.

questions.text: Which of the following is produced by the action of CH3CI on benzene in presence of AICI3 followed by oxidation in the presence of acidified KMnO4 give :

By the action of CH3Cl, Benzene in the presence of AlCl3 is converted to toluene by Friedal-Crafts reaction.Toluene then undergoes side chain oxidation in presence of acidified KMn04 to give benzoic acid ,hence this is not the correct option.

questions.explanation: All of the carbon atoms in the benzene rings are sp2-hybridized: the overlap of the sp2 orbitals around the ring produces a framework of six sigma bonds, while the unhybridized p-orbitals are perpendicular to this plane overlapping in a side-to-side fashion to form three pi-bonds.

questions.text: August Kekulé's structure of benzene failed to explain _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Kekule's structure does not explain the extra ordinary stable nature of benzene molecule and its lack of reactivity towards addition reactions, resistance towards oxidation etc.According to Kekule's structure benzene contains three double bonds and hence the chemical properties of benzene should resemble those of alkene, but in actuality, it is not so as Benzene is highly stable and forms substitution compounds easily.

questions.explanation: Kekule's structure does not explain the benzene molecule's extraordinarily stable nature, its lack of reactivity towards addition reactions, resistance towards oxidation, etc.According to Kekule's structure, benzene contains three double bonds, and hence, its chemical properties should resemble those of alkene. However, in actuality, this is not so, as Benzene is highly stable and forms substitution compounds easily.
